17th C. Baroque drawing - Virgin Mary on a Crescent Moon / Description: A finely executed red chalk drawing depicting the Virgin Mary standing on a crescent moon, trampling the serpent, and elevated by angels. This iconography represents the Immaculate Conception, a subject that enjoyed great popularity in 17th-century Spain and Italy during the Counter-Reformation. The composition follows traditional Baroque models with its dynamic drapery, swirling clouds, and numerous putti supporting the

Antony Wierix (1552-1604) - Blessing Christ Child / Description: Sitting in the grass, the plump Christ child blesses with his raised right hand. Giovanni Colmo-Turin 1867-1947, "Macugnaga"-Oil on panel cm 20x14 - Giovanni Colmo was an Italian painter of the 19th and 20th centuries, known for his dedication to landscape and for his self-taught training. Born in Turin on May 13, 1867, he was the older brother of Eugenio Colmo, a well-known caricaturist under the pseudonym "Goliath." The Russian Orthodox icon Ascension of the Lord is a true masterpiece of Russian Orthodox painting of the 19th century, executed in the best traditions of the Moscow school. The plot of the icon reflects a key moment in Christian history - the Ascension of Jesus Christ, symbolizing his divine ascension and victory over the earthly world. The icon of Saint Simeon the Stylite was created in Moscow at the turn of the 19th and 20th centuries and represents a characteristic synthesis of Academic painting and the refined silversmith tradition of the capital workshops. The image of the Protection of the Most Holy Theotokos. Midthird quarter of the 19th century. Possibly Vetka. The image, especially revered in Rus`, is a centric, Novgorod composition, in which the memory of getting rid of the Slavic raid on Constantinople in the 7th century is recorded. Then the weekly veneration of the protection of the Mother of God, which grew into a religious procession, saved the city. This solemnly fixes the upper case of the icon. European School 19th century (Style of the Italian Trecento/Quattrocento).This painting depicts the Madonna holding the Christ Child rendered in a style echoing the late Medieval and early Renaissance traditions of Southern Europe The central figures are set against a traditional gold leaf background featuring an incised decorative halo and border patterns The Virgin is draped in a dark hooded mantle over a red tunic a symbolic color palette representing both her humanity and divine sorrow . 18th century Italian painter - Holy Family.The painting depicts the three figures of the Virgin Mary, Jesus and Joseph against a dark background. The Virgin Mary is depicted in a three-quarter portrait in a blue-and-red robe and headdress, her loving gaze and shining eyes directed outward from the painting. Pietà, The Lamentation of Christ 18 th century .This evocative 18th-century polychrome wood carving represents the Pietà, a traditional subject in Christian art depicting the Virgin Mary cradling the dead body of Jesus. The Virgin is shown in a seated position, her face marked by quiet grief as she supports Christ’s limp, elongated form across her lap.

A MONUMENTAL ICON SHOWING THE ENTRY INTO JERUSALEM FROM A CHURCH ICONOSTASIS Russian, 16th century Tempera on wood panel with kovcheg. The icon originating in all probability from the Feast Day tier of an iconostasis. Inserted into another panel (vrezka), minimally restored. 74 x 49.5 cm.

Attached to the classicism of Nicolas Poussin in his early years, his paintings of both religious and secular subjects display original and delightful inventions. Ecce Homo, 15th/16th century h cm 72, base cm 40 Half-length polychrome wooden sculpture. This work rendered expressive with extreme psychological realism, shows Christ at the moment when, according to the Gospel of John, Pontius Pilate - governor of Rome - shows Christ to the crowd by uttering the famous phrase "Ecce Homo".The sculpture, made of wood, technically according to the Early Medieval usage, was emptied of the inner pith so that over time cracks and fissures would not appear.The work

UMBRIAN MASTER, 15TH CENTURY. Ecce Homo (The Man of Sorrows).This exquisite 15th-century panel is a sublime example of Umbrian devotional art, characterized by its lyrical tenderness and meditative stillness. Represented in the Imago Pietatis tradition, the figure of Christ emerges from a circular tondo—a geometric symbol of perfection and eternity—framed by the instruments of the Passion. The delicate modeling of the torso and the serene, downcast gaze reflect the profound influence of the

Italian School, 17th Century .The Holy Family with Saint John the Baptist.This painting, a fine example of the Italian Baroque period, depicts a serene and intimate scene of the Holy Family. The Virgin Mary is shown tenderly holding the Christ Child, who engages with the young Saint John the Baptist. Albrecht Durer (1471-1528) - Last Supper - Latin Text Edition - 1510 / Description: The Last Supper depicts Christ with his apostles. Judas is shown on the right, seen from behind, while on the left the innkeeper pours wine. This is one of the twelve woodcuts made by Albrecht Dürer for The Large Passion, published with Latin text in 1511. Circle of Jean-Baptiste Jouvenet (Rouen, 1644 - Paris, 1717). Last Supper, with frame. Oil on canvas. cm75x90 The work is to be related to the painting in the National Museum in Warsaw, believed to be a workshop copy of a work by Jouvenet exhibited at the Salon of 1704. The original is lost, but a drawing of the composition, kept at the Museum der bildenden Künste in Leipzig, is known, interpreted by scholar Antoine Schnapper as preparatory for the 1704 painting. Francesco Curradi (FLORENCE 1570 - 1661). Virgin Announced. Oil on canvas. 121x58 cm The painting is to be related to that of Francesco Curradi preserved in the Museo del Cenacolo di San Salvi in Florence. Curradi was one of the leading exponents of Florentine devotional painting in the first half of the 17th century, along with Matteo Rosselli. Workshop of Peter Paul Rubens (1577-1640) - Saint Jerome.This captivating oil painting, executed in the highly productive workshop of Peter Paul Rubens, depicts the revered Church Father, Saint Jerome. The composition is a masterful example of the Flemish Baroque style, characterized by its dynamic energy and dramatic use of light. The figure of Jerome, identifiable by his flowing white beard and robust physique, fills the frame. He is draped in a brilliant red cardinal's cloak, which provides a
